**CONTENTS OF THE CHAPTER**

* *Basic Concepts of Object-Oriented Programming: Classes, Objects, and Attributes*

* *Constructor Methods*

* *Getter and Setter Methods*

* *Object Comparison*

* *Arrays of Objects*

* *Overview of Common Built-in Classes in Java*

* *Inheritance, Polymorphism, and Abstraction in Object-Oriented Programming*

*Chapter 5 addresses a crucial aspect of Java programming: object-oriented programming. This chapter presents the fundamental concepts of object-oriented programming, including classes, attributes, objects, and constructors. It also discusses the core principles of object-oriented programming—inheritance, abstraction, polymorphism, and encapsulation—to help readers understand how object-oriented programming is applied in Java.*

# **5.1. OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING**

## **5.1.1. Basic Concepts**

**Object**\-**Oriented Programming** (**OOP**) is an application development method based on the concepts of **classes** and **objects** :

* **Object**: An entity that has state and behavior. Examples include bicycles, tables, chairs, etc. An object can represent either a physical or a logical entity.

* **Class**: A blueprint or template for creating objects. It is a logical entity that defines the properties and behaviors shared by its objects.

![][image1]

**Figure 5-1. Objects Created from a Template: the Car Class**

Object-oriented and procedural programming have many differences:

| ![][image2] |  | ![][image3] |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Figure 5-2. Procedure-oriented programming** |  | **Figure 5-3. Object-Oriented Programming** |

The advantage of object-oriented programming (OOP) over procedure-oriented programming is that it simplifies design, development, and maintenance. In contrast, procedure-oriented programming makes source code management difficult, especially for large projects. As a result, OOP improves the efficiency of the software development process. Additionally, OOP enables the modeling of real-world entities and behaviors, thereby making software development more intuitive and effective.

| Criteria | Procedural Programming | Object-Oriented Programming |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Programming Paradigm** | Procedural programming | Object-oriented programming |
| **Approach** | Top-down | Bottom-up |
| **Program Decomposition** | Program is divided into functions | Program is divided into classes and objects |
| **Design Structure** | Transform the program into functions | Divide the program into layers |
| **Access Control** | No access specification is provided | Access is defined using *public*, *private*, *protected*, and *default* |
| **Method Overloading and Polymorphism** | Not supported | Supported through method overloading and polymorphism |
| **Inheritance** | Inheritance is not supported | Inheritance is supported using *public*, *protected*, and *private* |
| **Data Sharing** | Data is shared among functions | Data is shared among objects if permitted |
| **Commonly Used Languages** | TC, VB, Pascal | C++, Java, C\#, Ruby |

## **5.1.2. Characteristics of Object-Oriented Programming**

![][image4]

**Figure 5-4. Illustration of objects in a video game.**

* **Inheritance**: Inheritance occurs when an object acquires the properties and behaviors of its parent object. This mechanism promotes code reusability.

* **Polymorphism**: Polymorphism refers to the ability of an object to take multiple forms, allowing it to exhibit different behaviors under different conditions.

* **Abstraction**: Abstraction is the process of hiding unnecessary implementation details and exposing only the essential features of an object, focusing on what an object does rather than how it does it.

* **Encapsulation**: Encapsulation is the practice of binding data and methods into a single unit (a class, a package) and restricting direct access to them. This helps protect the internal state of an object from external interference.

## **5.1.3. Some concepts about classes, objects, attributes, and methods**

* A **class** defines the structure and behavior of objects. It can also be considered a user-defined data type whose instances are treated as objects.  
* An **object** is an instance of a class.  
* An **attribute** is a property or characteristic that describes an object.  
* A **method** represents the behavior or actions that an object can perform.

Consider the following program segment, which defines a **Student** class using the `class` keyword:

| class Student {    String name;    int age;    public Student(String name, int age) {        this.name \= name;        this.age \= age;    }    public void display() {        System.*out*.println("Name: " \+ name);        System.*out*.println("Age: " \+ age);    }    public static void main(String\[\] args) {        Student s1 \= new Student("Linh", 24);        s1.display();    } } |
| :---- |

Result:

| Name: Linh Age: 24 |
| :---- |

The syntax for declaring a class is as follows:

| class \<class name\> { 	// Declare a list of attributes 	\<data\_type\> \<attribute\_name\>; 	... 	// Declare a list of methods 	public \<return type\> \<method name\>(\<parameter type\> \<parameter name\>, ...) { 		... 	} } |
| :---- |

Create a Student class with two attributes, name and age, and two methods, getInformation() and display(), as shown in the following table:

![][image5]

**Figure 5-5. Declaring attributes and methods for the Student class.**

| class Student {    String name;    int age;    public void getInformation() {    }    public void display() {    } } |
| :---- |

Use the keyword \`**new\`** to declare an object of a class. To declare an object of the **Student class** :

| Student s1 \= new Student( "Linh" , 24 ); s1.display (); |
| :---- |

Use the **"." operator** to access the properties and methods of an object.

| public class Program {    public static void main(String\[\] args) {        Circle c \= new Circle(7);        System.*out*.println(c.getArea());        System.*out*.println(c.getCircumference());    } } class Circle {    private double radius;    public Circle(double radius) {        this.radius \= radius;    }    public double getArea() {        return 3.14 \* radius \* radius;    }    public double getCircumference() {        return 3.14 \* 2 \* radius;    } } |
| :---- |

Result:

| 153.86 43.96 |
| :---- |

# **5.2. COMPARING TWO OBJECTS**

Consider the Student object:

| public class Student {    public String name;    public String address;    public Student(String name, String address) {        this.name \= name;        this.address \= address;    } } |
| :---- |

The Program class with the **main method** :

| public class Program {    public static void main(String\[\] args) {        Student s1 \= new Student("Viet", "Bac Ninh");        Student s2 \= new Student("Viet", "Bac Ninh");        System.*out*.println(s1 \== s2);    } } |
| :---- |

The results of the run are:

| false |
| :---- |

Although the two objects `s1` and `s2` are created with identical values for `name` and `address`, the expression `s1 == s2` returns `false`. This is because the `==` operator in Java compares **object references**, that is, the memory addresses where the objects are allocated, rather than the values of their attributes.

To compare object values, you must define a custom comparison method or override the `equals()` method inherited from the `Object` class, as shown in the following program:

| public class Student {    public String name ;    public String address ;    public Student ( String name, String address) {        this.name \= name ;        this.address \= address ; }    @Override    public boolean equals ( Object obj) {        return this . name \== (( Student ) obj). name && this . address \== (( Student ) obj). address ; } } |
| :---- |

The program class with the main method:

| public class Program {    public static void main ( String \[\] args) {        Student s1 \= new Student( "Viet" , "Bac Ninh" );        Student s2 \= new Student( "Viet" , "Bac Ninh" );        System . *out* .println( s1 .equals( s2 )); } } |
| :---- |

Result:

| True |
| :---- |

# **5.3. ACCESS MODIFIERS**

Access modifiers are **keywords** used before the declaration of a class, variable, or method to specify the level of access permitted to other classes.

For **classes**, Java provides two access levels: **public** and **default**.  
 For **variables and methods**, Java provides four access modifiers: **public**, **protected**, **default**, and **private**.

* **default** (no access modifier specified): Accessible within the same package.  
* **private**: Accessible only within the same class.  
* **public**: Accessible from anywhere.  
* **protected**: Accessible within the same package or by subclasses outside the package.

Summary table:

**Table 5-1. Summary table of access modifiers**

| Access Modifier | Same Class | Same Package | Subclasses Outside the Package | Other Classes Outside the Package |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **private** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **default** | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| **protected** | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| **public** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |

# **5.4. GETTER AND SETTER**

If a class attribute is declared as `public`, it can be accessed directly from outside the class. To reduce the use of public attributes and to protect the internal state of an object, attributes should be declared as `private`. Access to these attributes is then provided through public methods, commonly known as **getter** and **setter** methods.

* **Getter** methods provide controlled read access to private attributes.  
* **Setter** methods provide controlled write access to private attributes.

## **5.4.1. Getter**

**Getter** methods are methods that provide read access to a variable.

| public class Student {    private String name ;    private integer age ;    private double gpa ; } |
| :---- |

The following example provides read access to the variable `name`:

| public String getName (){    return name ; } |
| :---- |

By convention, the name of a getter method follows the form **`get` \+ variable name**, with the first letter of the variable name capitalized. For example: `getName()`.

## **5.4.2. Setter**

Setter methods provide "write access" to a private variable, allowing other classes to assign a new value to the variable.

| public void setName ( String name){    this.name \= name ; } |
| :---- |

By convention, the name of a setter method begins with **`set`** followed by the capitalized variable name, and it must accept a parameter of the same type as the variable.

# **5.5. CONSTRUCTOR METHOD**

## **5.5.1. Concept of Constructors Methods**

A **constructor** is a special method that is automatically invoked when an object is created. Its purpose is to initialize the newly created object.

Constructors have the following characteristics:

* A constructor has the **same name as the class**.

* A constructor **does not declare a return type**, not even `void`.

Unlike regular methods, which are invoked using the dot (`.`) operator, a constructor is invoked using the `new` operator.

Objects are created using the `new` operator. For example, the following program creates an object of the `Student` class:

| Student s1 ; //Declare a variable of type Student s1 \= new Student(); // Creates a new object and stores a reference to it. |
| :---- |

In this example, the expression `new Student()`:

* Allocates memory for the object,  
* Initializes the object’s instance variables,  
* Returns a reference to the newly created object.

After the assignment statement is executed, `s1` refers to the newly created object. The expression `Student()` resembles a method call; in fact, it is a call to a special method known as a **constructor**.

Every Java class has at least one constructor. If a programmer does not explicitly define a constructor, Java automatically provides a **default constructor** for that class.

Rules for Creating Constructors

* The constructor name must be **identical to the class name**.

* A constructor **has no return type**.

* A constructor cannot be declared as `abstract`, `static`, `final`, or `synchronized`.

* A constructor may be declared with access modifiers such as `public`, `protected`, or `private`.

* A constructor **cannot be static**.

In Java, constructors are classified into two types:

* **Parameterless (no-argument) constructors**

* **Parameterized constructors**

## **5.5.2. Parameterless constructor**

A **parameterless constructor** (also called a **no-argument constructor**) is a constructor that does not take any parameters. It is used to create an object with default initial values.

Example of a parameterless constructor in the `Student` class:

| public class Student {     String name;     int age;     public Student() {     } } |
| :---- |

Key points about parameterless constructors:

* A parameterless constructor **can be explicitly defined** by the programmer, as shown above.

* If a class **does not define any constructor**, Java automatically provides a **default parameterless constructor**.

* The automatically generated constructor initializes instance variables to their default values (e.g., `null` for objects, `0` for numeric types, `false` for `boolean`).

Once a constructor with parameters is explicitly defined, the system **no longer generates** the default parameterless constructor unless it is explicitly declared.

## **5.5.3. Parameterized constructors**

A **parameterized constructor** is a constructor that allows values to be passed to an object at the time of its creation. These values are used to initialize the instance variables of the class.

**Example:**

| public class Student {     String name;     int age;     public Student(String name, int age) {         this.name \= name;         this.age \= age;     } } |
| :---- |

Key points to note:

* A parameterized constructor enables **initialization of instance variables with specific values** at the time of object creation.  
* Once a constructor is explicitly declared in a class, the **default parameterless constructor is no longer automatically generated** by Java.  
* If a parameterless constructor is still required, it **must be explicitly defined** in the class.

# **5.6. OBJECT ARRAY**

Declaring and using arrays of objects differs from declaring and using arrays of primitive data types such as `int` or `double`. When working with an array of objects, each element of the array must be **explicitly initialized** before it can be used.

For example, suppose `Student` is a user-defined class. An array of type `Student[]` contains references to `Student` objects. To store information for 30 students, an array can be declared and initialized as follows:

| public static void main ( String \[\] args) {    // Declare an array of students with 30 elements *   *Student \[\] students \= new Student\[ 30 \];    for ( int i \= 0 ; i \< 30 ; i \++) {        // Initialize the elements in the students array *       *students \[ i \] \= new Student(); } } |
| :---- |

This array contains 30 elements: `students[0]`, `students[1]`, …, `students[29]`. After initialization, each `students[i]` references a `Student` object.

Consider the following definition of the `Student` class:

| public class Student {     String name;     int age;     public Student(String name, int age) {         this.name \= name;         this.age \= age;     }     public void display() {         System.out.println("Name: " \+ name);         System.out.println("Age: " \+ age);     } } |
| :---- |

The `Program` class demonstrates how to use an array of `Student` objects:

| public class Program {     public static void main(String\[\] args) {         // Declare an array of students with 3 elements         Student\[\] students \= new Student\[3\];         students\[0\] \= new Student("Thai", 12);         students\[1\] \= new Student("Tuan", 13);         students\[2\] \= new Student("Duc", 11);         for (int i \= 0; i \< 3; i++) {             students\[i\].display();         }     } } |
| :---- |

The output of the program is:

| Name: Thai Age: 12 Name: Tuan Age: 13 Name: Duc Age: 11 |
| :---- |

# **5.7. SOME COMMON CLASSES**

## **5.7.1. The java.lang.StringBuilder class**

The `StringBuilder` class, which belongs to the standard `java.lang` package, is used to efficiently build and modify strings composed of multiple parts. Unlike the `String` class, which is immutable, `StringBuilder` allows its content to be changed without creating new objects.

To use `StringBuilder`, you first create an instance of the class:

| StringBuilder builder \= new StringBuilder(); |
| :---- |

Similar to a `String`, a `StringBuilder` maintains a sequence of characters. A long string can be constructed by repeatedly appending smaller strings using the `append()` method. Once the construction is complete, the `toString()` method is used to obtain the final result as a `String` object.

The following example compares the execution time of string concatenation using the `String` class and the `StringBuilder` class:

| public class Program {     private static final String STR \= "A";     public static String stringConcatenation() {         String s \= STR;         for (int i \= 0; i \< 100000; i++)             s \= s \+ STR;         return s;     }     public static String stringBuilderConcatenation() {         StringBuilder sb \= new StringBuilder(STR);         for (int i \= 0; i \< 100000; i++)             sb.append(STR);         return sb.toString();     }     public static void main(String\[\] args) {         long start \= System.currentTimeMillis();         stringConcatenation();         long end \= System.currentTimeMillis();         System.out.println("Time taken to concatenate String: " \+ (end \- start) \+ " ms");         start \= System.currentTimeMillis();         stringBuilderConcatenation();         end \= System.currentTimeMillis();         System.out.println("Time taken to concatenate strings using StringBuilder: " \+ (end \- start) \+ " ms");     } } |
| :---- |

Result:

| Time taken to concatenate String: 3542 ms Time taken to concatenate strings using StringBuilder: 2 ms |
| :---- |

## **5.7.2. The java.util.Date class**

The `java.util.Date` class is used to represent a specific point in time, including both date and time information. When a `Date` object is created without any parameters, it represents the **current date and time**.

| public static void main ( String \[\] args) {    System . *out* .println( new Date()); } |
| :---- |

The output is displayed in a readable date–time format, for example:

| Sun Sep 12 07:58:44 ICT 2021 |
| :---- |

This output reflects the current date and time according to the system’s default time zone.

## **5.7.3. The java.lang.Math class**

The `Math` class provides a collection of **static methods** for performing common mathematical operations, such as trigonometric functions (`sin`, `cos`, `tan`), rounding methods, and random number generation.

For example, the `random()` method returns a pseudorandom value of type `double` in the range \[0.0,1.0)\[0.0, 1.0)\[0.0,1.0):

| double x \= Math.random() |
| :---- |

The following example defines a `PairOfDice` class. Each object of this class represents a pair of dice, using two instance variables to store the values shown on the dice. When a `PairOfDice` object is created, the dice are initialized with random values.

| public class PairOfDice {    public int dice1 , dice2 ;    public PairOfDice () { roll(); }    public void roll () {        dice1 \= ( int ) ( Math . *random* () \* 6 ) \+ 1 ;        dice2 \= ( int ) ( Math . *random* () \* 6 ) \+ 1 ;        System . *out* .println( "Dice 1 \= " \+ dice1 );        System . *out* .println( "Dice 2 \= " \+ dice2 ); }    public static void main ( String \[\] args) {        PairOfDice pairOfDice \= new PairOfDice(); } } |
| :---- |

A possible output of the program is:

| Dice 1 \= 5 Dice 2 \= 6 |
| :---- |

Note that, in addition to the `Math.random()` method, Java also provides the `java.util.Random` class, which can be used to create a `Random` object for generating random numbers with greater flexibility and control.

# **5.8. INHERITANCE**

In object-oriented programming, **inheritance** is the mechanism by which one class acquires the attributes and methods of another class. A class that inherits attributes and methods from another class is called a **derived class** or **subclass**, while the class from which the attributes and methods are inherited is called a **base class** or **parent class**.

![A computer screen shot of a computer screenDescription automatically generated][image6]

**Table 5-2. The Teacher and Student classes both inherit from the Person class.**

In Java, inheritance is implemented using the `extends` keyword:

| class B extends A {     // Additions and modifications     // Content inherited from class A } |
| :---- |

For example:

| public class Student extends Person { } public class Teacher extends Person { } |
| :---- |

The `Student` and `Teacher` classes inherit common attributes and methods from the `Person` class. In this relationship, `Person` is the **parent (base) class**, while `Student` and `Teacher` are **child (derived) classes**.

In Java inheritance:

* **Private** attributes and methods of a parent class are **not directly accessible** by subclasses.  
* Subclasses can inherit attributes and methods declared as **public**, **protected**, or **default** (when the subclass and parent class are in the same package).  
* Access to a parent class’s private members must be provided through **getter and setter methods**.  
* A class declared with the `final` keyword **cannot be inherited**.

# **5.9. OVERLOADING METHODS **

In inheritance, a subclass can **override** a method of its parent class to provide a specific implementation. This is illustrated in the following program:

| class SuperClass {     public void display() {         System.out.println("Hello from SuperClass");     } } class SubClass extends SuperClass {     @Override     public void display() {         System.out.println("Hello from SubClass");     } } class Entry {     public static void main(String\[\] args) {         SubClass s \= new SubClass();         s.display();     } } |
| :---- |

The output of the program is:

| Hello from SubClass |
| :---- |

In this example, the `display()` method of `SuperClass` is **overridden** by the `display()` method in `SubClass`. When the method is invoked on a `SubClass` object, the overridden version in the subclass is executed.

The `@Override` annotation indicates that the method is intended to override a method in the parent class. Although its use is not mandatory, it is strongly recommended for the following reasons:

* The compiler verifies that the annotated method correctly overrides a method in the parent class and reports an error if it does not.  
* It improves code readability by clearly indicating that the method overrides a superclass method.

Note that a method declared with the `final` keyword **cannot be overridden** by subclasses.

# **5.10. THE JAVA.LANG.OBJECT CLASS**

Every class in Java implicitly inherits from the `Object` class. If a class is declared without explicitly extending another class, it automatically becomes a subclass of the special `Object` class, which is defined in the `java.lang` package.

The `Object` class provides several methods that are inherited by all Java classes.

Methods of the `Object` Class:

| Method | Description |
| ----- | ----- |
| `public final Class<?> getClass()` | Returns the runtime class of the object. |
| `public int hashCode()` | Returns a hash code value for the object. |
| `public boolean equals(Object obj)` | Compares this object with another object for equality. |
| `protected Object clone() throws CloneNotSupportedException` | Creates and returns a copy of the object. |
| `public String toString()` | Returns a string representation of the object. |
| `public final void notify()` | Wakes up a single thread waiting on this object. |
| `public final void notifyAll()` | Wakes up all threads waiting on this object. |
| `public final void wait()` throws InterruptedException | Causes the current thread to wait until it is notified. |
| `public final void wait(long timeout)` throws InterruptedException | Causes the current thread to wait for a specified time. |
| `public final void wait(long timeout, int nanos)` throws InterruptedException | Causes the current thread to wait for a specified time with nanosecond precision. |
| `protected void finalize() throws Throwable` | Called by the garbage collector before the object is reclaimed. |

A class can **override** methods inherited from the `Object` class to provide behavior specific to that class. A commonly overridden method is `toString()`.

The following example overrides the `toString()` method in the `PairOfDice` class introduced earlier: 

| public class PairOfDice {     public int dice1, dice2;     public PairOfDice() {         roll();     }     @Override     public String toString() {         if (dice1 \== dice2)             return "Double " \+ dice1;         else             return dice1 \+ " and " \+ dice2;     }     public void roll() {         dice1 \= (int)(Math.random() \* 6\) \+ 1;         dice2 \= (int)(Math.random() \* 6\) \+ 1;     }     public static void main(String\[\] args) {         PairOfDice pairOfDice \= new PairOfDice();         System.out.println(pairOfDice.toString());     } } |
| :---- |

In this example, the overridden `toString()` method replaces the inherited version from the `Object` class, providing a more meaningful string representation of a `PairOfDice` object.

# **5.11. THIS AND SUPER**

## **5.11.1. The keyword "this"**

The keyword `this` is an important feature in Java that refers to the **current object**. It is commonly used to access the object’s instance variables and methods.

The `this` keyword is especially useful for distinguishing between **instance variables** and **method parameters** when they share the same name. This situation frequently occurs in constructor methods.

Consider the following example:

| public class Circle {     private double radius;     public Circle(double radius) {         radius \= radius;     }     public static void main(String\[\] args) {         Circle circle \= new Circle(3.12);         System.out.println("radius \= " \+ circle.radius);     } } |
| :---- |

The output of the program is:

| radius \= 0 |
| :---- |

In this example, the constructor parameter `radius` has the same name as the instance variable `radius`. Inside the constructor, the assignment `radius = radius` assigns the parameter to itself rather than to the instance variable. As a result, the instance variable remains unchanged and retains its default value (`0.0` for type `double`).

To resolve this ambiguity, the `this` keyword is used to explicitly refer to the instance variable of the current object.

The corrected program is shown below:

| public class Circle {     private double radius;     public Circle(double radius) {         this.radius \= radius;     }     public static void main(String\[\] args) {         Circle circle \= new Circle(3.12);         System.out.println("radius \= " \+ circle.radius);     } } |
| :---- |

Run results:

| radius \= 3.12 |
| :---- |

By using `this.radius`, the constructor correctly assigns the parameter value to the instance variable of the object.

## **5.11.2. The keyword "super"**

The keyword `super` is used to refer to members of a **parent (superclass)** from within a **child (subclass)**. It is commonly used to resolve name conflicts when a subclass overrides a method defined in its parent class.

When a subclass defines a method with the same name as a method in its superclass, the subclass method **overrides** the superclass method. However, the `super` keyword can be used to explicitly invoke the overridden method of the parent class.

Consider the following example:

| class Animal { // Superclass (parent)     public void animalSound() {         System.out.println("The animal makes a sound");     } } class Dog extends Animal { // Subclass (child)     @Override     public void animalSound() {         super.animalSound();         System.out.println("The dog says: bow wow");     } } |
| :---- |

Main program:

| public static void main(String\[\] args) {     Animal myDog \= new Dog();     myDog.animalSound(); } |
| :---- |

The output of the program is:

| The animal makes a sound The dog says: bow wow |
| :---- |

In this example, the `animalSound()` method in the `Dog` class overrides the method in the `Animal` class. The call to `super.animalSound()` ensures that the parent class’s version of the method is executed before the subclass’s additional behavior.

# **5.14. ABSTRACT**

Abstraction is a fundamental concept in object-oriented programming that focuses on exposing only the **essential features** of an object while hiding unnecessary implementation details. In Java, abstraction is achieved through **abstract classes** and **interfaces**.

A class declared with the `abstract` keyword **cannot be instantiated**. It is intended to be used only as a superclass for other classes.

Example:

| abstract class Animal { } class Cat extends Animal { } |
| :---- |

The statement `Animal a = new Cat();` is valid, because a reference of an abstract class type can refer to an object of a subclass. However, attempting to create an object using `new Animal()` will result in a compilation error, since abstract classes cannot be instantiated.

An abstract class may contain **abstract methods**. An abstract method is declared without an implementation body.

Example:

| abstract class Animal {    public abstract void makeSound (); } |
| :---- |

If a class inherits from an abstract class, it **must override all abstract methods**, unless the subclass itself is declared abstract.

Example:

| class Cat extends Animal {    @Override    public void makeSound () {        System . *out* .println( "meow meow" ); } } |
| :---- |

A `Cat` object can then be created, and the overridden method can be invoked as follows:

| public class Program {    public static void main ( String \[\] args) {        Animal a \= new Cat();        a.makeSound (); } } |
| :---- |

The output of the program is:

| meow meow |
| :---- |

This example demonstrates how abstraction allows programs to work with general types while relying on specific implementations provided by subclasses.

# **5.12. INTERFACE**

An interface is used to define a set of abstract behaviors and constants that a class can implement. Interfaces support abstraction and help achieve multiple inheritance of behavior in Java.

Key characteristics of interfaces include:

* An interface is declared using the `interface` keyword.  
* Interfaces **cannot be instantiated**.  
  Interfaces are implemented by classes or extended by other interfaces.  
* All methods in an interface are **abstract by default**, except for `default` and `static` methods.  
* All variables declared in an interface are implicitly **public**, **static**, and **final** (constants).  
* The `abstract` keyword is not required when declaring abstract methods.  
* The `static` and `final` keywords are not required when declaring constants.

For example, the `IAnimal` interface can be defined as follows:

| public interface IAnimal {    default void move () {        System . *out* .println( "Moving" ); }    void sound (); int numberOfAnimal \= 0; } |
| :---- |

A class can inherit from multiple interfaces. Because Java is a language designed for simplicity, it doesn't support multiple inheritance with classes, but due to the nature of interfaces containing only abstract (empty) methods, Java allows a class to implement multiple interfaces.

| interface IFlyable {    void fly (); } interface IEatable {    void eat (); } class Bird implements IFlyable , IEatable {    @Override    public void fly () {        System . *out* .println( "Bird flying" ); }    @Override    public void eat () {        System . *out* .println( "Bird eats" ); } } |
| :---- |

Main program:

| public class Program {    public static void main ( String \[\] args) {        Bird bird \= new Bird();        bird.eat ();        bird.fly (); } } |
| :---- |

The result when running the program:

| Bird eats Bird flying |
| :---- |

**Note** : To implement an interface, use the keyword **\`implements\`** instead of **\`extends\`** . When looking at an interface, the only thing visible are the abstract methods (features); therefore, using an interface is considered completely abstract. Interfaces are usually named with a capital I at the beginning, often followed by a noun or adjective.

# **5.13. POLYMORPHISM**

Polymorphism refers to the ability of an object to take **multiple forms** and to exhibit different behaviors depending on the actual object type at runtime. In Java, polymorphism is primarily achieved through **method overriding** and **dynamic method binding**.

Consider the following example.

| class Animal {     public void sound() {         System.out.println("some sound");     } } // The Dog class inherits from Animal class Dog extends Animal {     @Override     public void sound() {         System.out.println("bow bow");     } } // The Cat class inherits from Animal class Cat extends Animal {     @Override     public void sound() {         System.out.println("meow meow");     } } // The Duck class inherits from Animal class Duck extends Animal {     @Override     public void sound() {         System.out.println("quack quack");     } } |
| :---- |

Main program:

| public class Program {     public static void main(String\[\] args) {         Animal animal \= new Animal();         animal.sound();         animal \= new Dog();         animal.sound();         animal \= new Cat();         animal.sound();         animal \= new Duck();         animal.sound();     } } |
| :---- |

The output of the program is:

| some game bow bow meow meow quack quack |
| :---- |

In this example:

* The reference variable `animal` is of type `Animal`.

* At runtime, it can refer to objects of different subclasses such as `Dog`, `Cat`, and `Duck`.

* Although the reference type remains the same, the method implementation that is executed depends on the **actual object type**.

This behavior demonstrates **runtime polymorphism**, where the same method call (`sound()`) results in different behaviors based on the object being referenced.

To determine whether an object belongs to a specific class or subclass, Java provides the `instanceof` keyword.

| public class Program {     public static void main(String\[\] args) {         Animal animal \= new Dog();         if (animal instanceof Dog) {             System.out.println("animal is an instance of the Dog class");         }     } } |
| :---- |

Key points about `instanceof`:

* It returns `true` if the object is an instance of the specified class or its subclasses.

* It is commonly used when type-specific behavior is required.

* Excessive use of `instanceof` may indicate poor design; polymorphism is generally preferred over explicit type checking.

Polymorphism improves **code flexibility**, **extensibility**, and **maintainability**, allowing programs to be written in a more general and reusable manner.

# **PRACTICE**

## **Lesson 1\. Inheritance**

Create a Person class:

| public class Person {    private int id ;    private String name ;    private integer age ;    private String address ;    public Person ( int id, String name, int age, String address) {        this.id \= id ;        this.name \= name ;        this.age \= age ;        this.address \= address ; }    public int getId () {        return id ; }    public void setId ( int id) {        this.id \= id ; }    public String getName () {        return name ; }    public void setName ( String name) {        this.name \= name ; }    public int getAge () {        return age ; }    public void setAge ( int age) {        this.age \= age ; }    public String getAddress () {        return address ; }    public void setAddress ( String address) {        this.address \= address ; } } |
| :---- |

Create a Student class that inherits from the Person class:

| public class Student extends Person {   int gpa ;    public Student ( int id, String name, int age, String address, int gpa) {        super (id, name, age, address);        this.gpa \= gpa ; }    public int getGpa () {        return gpa ; }    public void setGpa ( int gpa) {        this.gpa \= gpa ; } } |
| :---- |

Create a Teacher class that inherits from the Person class:

| public class Teacher extends Person {    Private income salary ;    public Teacher ( int id, String name, int age, String address, int salary) {        super (id, name, age, address);        this.salary \= salary ; }    public int getSalary () {        return salary ; }    public void setSalary ( int salary) {        this.salary \= salary ; } } |
| :---- |

## **Lesson 2\. Inheritance**

Create a base class named Circle and another class named Cylinder that inherits from Circle. Then, write a program to use these classes. The program diagram is as follows:

![][image7]

Create a Circle class as follows:

| public class Circle {    private double radius ;    private String color ; *   *public Circle() {        radius \= 1.0 ;        color \= "red" ; } *   *public Circle( double r) {        radius \= r;        color \= "red" ; } *   *public double getRadius() {        return radius ; } *   *public double getArea() {        return radius \* radius \* Math. *PI* ; } } |
| :---- |

Create a Cylinder class that inherits from the Circle class:

| public class Cylinder extends Circle {    private double height ; *   *public Cylinder() {        super (); *       *height \= 1.0 ; } *   *public Cylinder( double height) {        super (); // call superclass no-arg constructor Circle() *       *this.height \= height ; } *   *public Cylinder( double radius, double height) {        super (radius); // call superclass constructor Circle(r) *       *this.height \= height ; } *   *public double getHeight() {        return height ; } *   *public double getVolume() {        return getArea() \* height ; } } |
| :---- |

Create a TestCylinder class to test the classes you have created:

| public class TestCylinder { //save as "TestCylinder.java" *   *public static void main(String\[\] args) { *       *Cylinder c1 \= new Cylinder(); System. *out* .println( "Cylinder:"                + " radius=" \+ c1.getRadius() \+ " height=" \+ c1.getHeight() \+ " base area=" \+ c1.getArea() \+ " volume=" \+ c1.getVolume()); *       *Cylinder c2 \= new Cylinder( 10.0 ); System. *out* .println( "Cylinder:"                + " radius=" \+ c2.getRadius() \+ " height=" \+ c2.getHeight() \+ " base area=" \+ c2.getArea() \+ " volume=" \+ c2.getVolume()); *       *Cylinder c3 \= new Cylinder( 2.0 , 10.0 ); System. *out* .println( "Cylinder:"                + " radius=" \+ c3.getRadius() \+ " height=" \+ c3.getHeight() \+ " base area=" \+ c3.getArea() \+ " volume=" \+ c3.getVolume()); } } |
| :---- |

Execute the program. The result when running the program is as follows:

| Cylinder: radius=1.0 height=1.0 base area=3.141592653589793 volume=3.141592653589793 Cylinder: radius=1.0 height=10.0 base area=3.141592653589793 volume=31.41592653589793 Cylinder: radius=2.0 height=10.0 base area=12.566370614359172 volume=125.66370614359172 |
| :---- |

## **Lesson 3\. Practice**

Create a program to work with two types of employees:

* **Permanent staff** : receive a fixed salary.

* **Contract employees** : have a fixed salary and working hours.

The program's class diagram is as follows:

![A diagram of a workflowDescription automatically generated][image8]

Create the Employee class by following these steps:

| public class Employee { } |
| :---- |

Declare a variable to store employee names:

| String emptName ; |
| :---- |

Create two constructors:

| public Employee() { } public Employee(String name) {    empName \= name; } |
| :---- |

Create a method to display the name:

| void displayDetails() { System. *out* .printf( "Employee Name: %s" , empName ); } |
| :---- |

Create a SalaryEmployee class that inherits from the Employee class:

| public class SalaryEmployee extends Employee { } |
| :---- |

Declare a variable called \`salary\` to store employee salaries:

| double salary ; |
| :---- |

Declare constructor methods:

| public SalaryEmployee() { } public SalaryEmployee(String name, double currentSalary) {    super (name);    salary \= currentSalary; } |
| :---- |

Methods to display employee salary details:

| void displayDetails() {    super.displayDetails (); System. *out* .printf( " \\n " \+ empName \+ "'s Salary: %.2f \\n " , salary ); } |
| :---- |

WageEmployee inherits from the Employee class:

| public class WageEmployee extends Employee { } |
| :---- |

Declare additional attributes for the WageEmployee class:

| double rate ; int hours ; |
| :---- |

Declare two additional constructors:

| public WageEmployee() { } public WageEmployee(String name, double wageRate, int wageHours) {    super (name);    rate \= wageRate;    hours \= wageHours; } |
| :---- |

Create a method to display employee salary details:

| void displayDetails() {    super.displayDetails (); System. *out* .printf( " \\n " \+ empName \+ "'s Salary: %.2f \\n " , rate \* hours ); } |
| :---- |

Create a TestEmployee class with a main method to execute the main program:

| public class TestEmployee {    public static void main(String\[\] args) { Employee objJohn \= new SalaryEmployee( "John" , 2300.50 ); objJohn.displayDetails(); Employee objDavid \= new WageEmployee( "David" , 34.50 , 10 ); objDavid.displayDetails(); } } |
| :---- |

Execute the program and print the output:

| Employee Name: John John's Salary: 2300.50 Employee Name: David David's Salary: 345.00 |
| :---- |

# **QUIZ**

| The keyword \`super\` is used for: |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Reference to the interface |  |
| Reference to a subclass |  |
| Reference to the parent class |  |
| Reference to a superobject |  |

| To check if an object is an instance of a class, use: |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| instanceof |  |
| typeof |  |
| isclass |  |
| classof |  |

| The keyword abstract is used to: |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Abstracting a block of code |  |
| Create abstract classes or methods. |  |
| Preventing inheritance |  |
| Do not allow access to the property. |  |

| To have one class inherit from another, use the keyword: |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Extend |  |
| implements |  |
| abstract |  |
| inherit |  |

| To prevent a property from being accessed outside the class, use the keyword: |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| private |  |
| master |  |
| lockaccess |  |
| By default |  |

| The protected access setting is used for: |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Protect a property from change. |  |
| Allow the inheriting class to access |  |
| Prevent access by classes within the same package. |  |
| Prevent access by subclasses. |  |

| Getter and setter methods are used for: |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Get and set the value for the object. |  |
| Set access permissions |  |
| Prevent access to variables |  |
| Customize property access permissions. |  |

| Which statement about initialization methods is incorrect? |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| The constructor has the same name as the class. |  |
| The constructor method does not allow inheritance. |  |
| A constructor must always be accompanied by a destructor. |  |
| A class can have multiple constructors. |  |

| Polymorphism is characterized by: |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| An object can only have one unique behavior. |  |
| An object can inherit from multiple sources. |  |
| An object can take many different forms. |  |
| One object can be related to many other objects. |  |

| What output will be displayed when the following program is run ? class Person { private String name ;     public String getName () {         if ( name .length()\> 16 )             return "Name is too long\!";         Elle return name ; }     public void setName ( String name) {         if (name\!= null && name.length()\> 2 )             this.name \= name ; } } public class Program { public static void main ( String \[\] args) {         Person person \= new Person();         person.setName ( "Nguyen Van Thanh" );         System . *out* .println( person .getName()); } }  |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| An exception occurred. |  |
| A compilation error occurred. |  |
| The screen displays "Name is too long". |  |
| Nguyen Van Thanh's display screen |  |

| What happens when you compile/run the following code ? interface MyInterface {     int *x* \= 1 ; } public class MyClass { public static void main ( String \[\] args) {         MyInterface . *x* \= 2 ;         System . *out* .println( MyInterface . *x* ); } }  |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Print the value 1\. |  |
| Print the value 2\. |  |
| Compilation error |  |
| Error while running |  |

|  |
| :---- |

| What is the output of the following code snippet ? class Parent { private String name ; private integer age ;     public Parent () {} } class Child extends Parent {     public Child () {         name \= "John" ;         age \= 42 ; } public static void main ( String \[\] args) {         Child child \= new Child();         System . *out* .println( child ); } }  |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Compilation error |  |
| John 42 |  |
| {name:"John", age:42} |  |
| Child@xxxxxx where xxxxxx is a sequence of numbers |  |

| What is the output of the following code snippet ? public class Program {     int field1, field2; String field3 ;     public Program ( int i, int j, String k) {         field1 \= i;         field2 \= j;         field3 \= k; } public static void main ( String \[\] args) {         Program foo1 \= new Program( 0 , 0 , "bar" );         Program foo2 \= new Program( 0 , 0 , "bar" );         System . *out* .println( foo1 .equals( foo2 )); *// prints false* } }  |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| false |  |
| true |  |
| Compilation error |  |
| The program makes an exception. |  |

| What is the output of the following code snippet ? public interface MyInterface {     void method1 ();     int method2 ();     String *TEXT* \= "Hello" ;     int *ANSWER* \= 42 ;     static void main ( String \[\] args) {         System . *out* .println( *TEXT* ); } }  |  |
| :---- | :---- |
| Print the word "Hello" |  |
| Compilation error |  |
| The program makes an exception. |  |
| Print the text TEXT |  |

# **EXERCISES**

## **Lesson 1\. Practice on Abstraction**

The entire source code is written in a single class called Media, which includes the main method and the Item, MP3, and Book classes:

Create an Item class:

* It is an abstract class.

* It has a property containing a name (public) of type String.

* There is a description property (private) of type String that has a corresponding get set method.

* It has an attribute containing an ID (protected) of type String.

* It has a price attribute (default) of type float or int.

* There is an abstract method void showInfo().

* There is a constructor that takes the aforementioned attributes as parameters.

Create an MP3 layer:

* Inherit (extend) the Item class.

* There is an additional attribute called duration.

* There is a constructor with all the necessary attributes.

Create a Book class:

* Create a Book class that inherits from the Item class.

* There are additional properties: author (String), numberOfPages(int), genre (String).

* There is a constructor with all the necessary attributes.

Main program function

* Write a main() method that creates an MP3 object and a Book object.

* Call the showInfo() method of each object to display the object's attribute information.

## **Lesson 2\. Practice on Polymorphism**

Write code in the abstract class **Shape** , paying attention to completing the class by adding code to some methods.

| public abstract class Shape {    private String color ;    private boolean protected ; Shape() { } Shape(String color, boolean filled) { } String getColor() { }    void setColor(String color) { }    boolean isFilled() { }    void setFilled( boolean filled) { }    abstract double getArea();    abstract double getPerimeter();    public abstract String toString(); } |
| :---- |

Write code in the **Circle class** , paying attention to completing the class by **adding code** to some methods.

| public class Circle extends Shape {    private double radius ; Circle() { } Circle ( double radius) { } Circle( double radius, String color, boolean filled) { }    public double getRadius() { }    void setRadius( double radius) { }    double getArea() { }    double getPerimeter() { }    public String toString() { } } |
| :---- |

Write code in the **Rectangle class** , paying attention to completing the class by **adding code** to some methods.

| public class Rectangle extends Shape {    private double width ;    private double length ; Rectangle() { }    @Override    double getArea() { }    @Override    double getPerimeter() { }    @Override    public String toString() { }    public double getWidth() { }    public void setWidth( double width) { }    public double getLength() { }    public void setLength( double length) {       } } |
| :---- |

Write code in the **Square class** , paying attention to completing the class by **adding code** to some methods. Add code to the Square class.

| public class Square extends Rectangle { } |
| :---- |

Use the code below to test all the classes created above   
. There are some errors in this code; explain them, fix them, and run the program.

| public class TestAllShape {    public static void main(String\[\] args) { Shape s1 \= new Circle( 5.5 , "RED" , false ); System.out.println *(* s1); System. *out* .println(s1.getArea()); System. *out* .println(s1.getPerimeter()); System. *out* .println(s1.getColor()); System. *out* .println(s1.isFilled()); System. *out* .println(s1.getRadius()); Circle c1 \= (Circle)s1; System.out.println *(* c1); System. *out* .println(c1.getArea()); System. *out* .println(c1.getPerimeter()); System. *out* .println(c1.getColor()); System. *out* .println(c1.isFilled()); System. *out* .println(c1.getRadius()); Shape s2 \= new Shape(); Shape s3 \= new Rectangle( 1.0 , 2.0 , "RED" , false ); *// Upcast* *       *System.out.println *(* s3); System. *out* .println(s3.getArea()); System. *out* .println(s3.getPerimeter()); System. *out* .println(s3.getColor()); System. *out* .println(s3.getLength()); Rectangle r1 \= (Rectangle)s3; *//downcast* *       *System.out.println *(* r1); System. *out* .println(r1.getArea()); System. *out* .println(r1.getColor()); System. *out* .println(r1.getLength()); Shape s4 \= new Square( 6.6 ); *// Upcast* *       *System.out.println *(* s4); System. *out* .println(s4.getArea()); System. *out* .println(s4.getColor()); System. *out* .println(s4.getSide()); Rectangle r2 \= (Rectangle)s4; System.out.println *(* r2); System. *out* .println(r2.getArea()); System. *out* .println(r2.getColor()); System. *out* .println(r2.getSide()); System. *out* .println(r2.getLength()); Square sq1 \= (Square)sq1; System.out.println *(* sq1); System. *out* .println(sq1.getArea()); System. *out* .println(sq1.getColor()); System. *out* .println(sq1.getSide()); System. *out* .println(sq1.getLength()); } } |
| :---- |

Please refer to the following class diagram:

![A diagram of a basic shapeDescription automatically generated with medium confidence][image9]

## **Lesson 3\. Practice on Interfaces**

Design a Shape interface and define the basic behaviors that other classes will implement. The class diagram will look like this:

![A diagram of a triangle and rectangleDescription automatically generated][image10]

## **Lesson 4\. Practical exercises on object-oriented programming in general.**

Write code to create a Shape class with the following variables, constructor, and methods:

| private String color ; public Shape(String color) {    this.color \= color ; } public String toString() {    return "Shape of color= \\" " \+ color \+ " \\" " ; } public double getArea() { System.err.println ( "I don't know what shape it is, *so* I can't calculate the area" );    return 0 ; // Need a return to compile the program } |
| :---- |

Create a Rectangle class that inherits from the Shape class:

| public class Rectangle extends Shape{    } |
| :---- |

Declare additional properties for the Rectangle class:

| private int length ; private int width ; |
| :---- |

Write the constructor:

| public Rectangle(String color, int length, int width) {    super (heat);    this.length \= length ;    this.width \= width ; } |
| :---- |

Two methods override methods from the parent class:

| @Override public String toString() { return "Rectangle of length=" \+ length \+ " and width=" \+ width \+ ", subclass of " \+ super.toString(); } @Override public double getArea() { return length \* width ; } |
| :---- |

Create a Triangle class that inherits from the Shape class:

| public class Triangle extends Shape { } |
| :---- |

Declare additional attributes for the Triangle class:

| private int base ; private int height ; |
| :---- |

Write the constructor:

| public Triangle(String color, int base, int height) {    super (heat);    this.base \= base ;    this.height \= height ; } |
| :---- |

Two methods override methods from the parent class:

| @Override public String toString() {    return "Triangle of base=" \+ base \+ " and height=" \+ height \+ ", subclass of " \+ super .toString(); } @Override public double getArea() {    return 0.5 \* base \* height ; } |
| :---- |

Create a TestShape class as the main program, and write the code for the main() method:

| Shape s; s \= new Rectangle( "red" , 4 , 5 ); System.out.println *(* s); System. *out* .println( "Area is " \+ s.getArea()); s \= new Triangle( "blue" , 4 , 5 ); System.out.println *(* s); System. *out* .println( "Area is " \+ s.getArea()); s \= new Shape( "green" ); System.out.println *(* s); System. *out* .println( "Area is " \+ s.getArea()); |
| :---- |

Run the main program and check the results:

| Rectangle of length=4 and width=5, subclass of Shape of color="red" Area is 20.0 Triangle of base=4 and height=5, subclass of Shape of color="blue" Area is 10.0 Shape of color="green" Area is 0.0 |
| :---- |

## **Lesson 5\. Practice on Interfaces and Method Overloading**

Given the class diagram of the program shown below:

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image11]

Create an interface named Moveable:

| interface Moveable { } |
| :---- |

Declare abstract methods in the Moveable interface:

| Void moveUp(); void moveDown(); void moveLeft(); Void moveRight(); |
| :---- |

Create a MovablePoint class that implements the Movable Interface:

| public class MoveablePoint implements Movable {    @Override    public void moveUp() { }    @Override    public void moveDown() { }    @Override    public void moveLeft() { }    @Override    public void moveRight() { } } |
| :---- |

Write a program for the MovablePoint class. Declare the variable (x, y) \- the coordinates of the point:

| private int x ; private int y ; |
| :---- |

Initialization method:

| public MoveablePoint( int x, int y) {    this.x \= x ;    this.y \= y ; } public String toString() {    return "Point at (" \+ x \+ "," \+ y \+ ")" ; } |
| :---- |

Write code to implement the abstract methods defined by Movable:

| @Override public void moveUp() {    y \--; } @Override public void moveDown() {    y \++; } @Override public void moveLeft() {    x \--; } @Override public void moveRight() {    x \++; } |
| :---- |

Create the main program class with the main method:

| public static void main(String\[\] args) { Moveable m1 \= new MoveablePoint( 5 , 5 ); System.out.println *(* m1); m1.moveDown(); System.out.println *(* m1); m1.moveRight(); System.out.println *(* m1); } |
| :---- |

Running the main program, the result:

| Point at (5,5) Point at (5,6) Point at (6,6) |
| :---- |

**REFERENCES**

\[1\] Core Java: Fundamentals (2021) , Cay Horstmann (Oracle Press Java).

\[2\] Head First Java: A Brain-Friendly Guide (2022), Kathy Sierra, O'Reilly Media.

\[3\] Java OOP Done Right: Create object oriented code you can be proud of with modern Java Paperback (2019), Mr Alan Mellor, Mellor Books.

\[4\] Murach's Java Programming (5th Edition) (2017), Joe Murach, Mike Murach & Associates.

\[5\]. Java for Absolute Beginners Learn to Program the Fundamentals the Java 9+ Way.

\[6\]. Modern Java Recipes: Simple Solutions to Difficult Problems in Java 8 and 9 (2017), by Ken Kousen, O'Reilly Media.

\[7\] Effective Java (2018), Joshua Bloch, Addison-Wesley Professional.
